# Medical Education

Research and news on artificial intelligence in medical education.

Items are selected nightly by an automated pipeline and link to their original sources. See [About](../about.md) for how selection works.

- **[Re-Centering Clinical Documentation in the Age of AI Scribes: Four Aims of the Patient Chart Note](https://mededu.jmir.org/2026/1/e95218)**  
  *JMIR Medical Education, June 8, 2026.* Clinical documentation is a foundational skill in medicine, developed during training and required in everyday practice. Historically, the chart note functioned as a clinician-centered cognitive tool for reasoning, teaching, and communication but has evolved into a multipurpose document shaped by administrative, regulatory, and financial demands, and is increasingly experienced as burdensome.   *JMIR Medical Education, June 8, 2026.* Background: For novice anatomy learners, studying human anatomy using textbooks, 2D learning materials, and static anatomical models frequently causes challenges in understanding complex anatomical structures. Since access to dissected human donor bodies is limited in many premedical programs, researchers are concerned with exploring novel supplementary approaches to anatomy learning. - **[AI Integration in Spanish Undergraduate Medical Education: National Cross-Sectional Study](https://mededu.jmir.org/2026/1/e88511)**  
  *JMIR Medical Education, June 8, 2026.* Background: Artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping clinical practice and redefining the competencies future physicians will need. - **[Adoption and Use of Social Media in Health Care Among Medical Residents: Cross-Sectional Study](https://mededu.jmir.org/2026/1/e83475)**  
  *JMIR Medical Education, June 5, 2026.* Background: Social media apps are widely used by health care professionals despite security and regulatory risks. - **[AI-Assisted Formative Assessment in Clinical Education: From Algorithms to Agency](https://mededu.jmir.org/2026/1/e93710)**  
  *JMIR Medical Education, June 4, 2026.* Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly reshaping clinical education by embedding assessment and feedback into everyday learning activities. Medical students can now use machine learning dashboards, generative AI, large language models, and emerging agentic systems to practice clinical reasoning, communication, and procedural skills while receiving individualized feedback within seconds.
